title: Making a leather tool roll
subtitle: Cut and stitch a wax-finished leather roll with individual tool pockets
excerpt: How to make a leather tool roll: cutting the main panel and pocket strip, stitching the divided pockets, attaching the tie strap, and finishing the edges. The finished roll holds 8 to 12 tools and rolls tight for storage.
sourceNotes: Traditional craft sources. Al Stohlman: The Art of Hand Sewing Leather.

content.text: is cut from 2 to 2.5 mm vegetable-tanned leather; the pocket strip from 1.5 mm. The
are planned around the tools the roll must hold. Lay the tools flat and measure the total width to determine the spine width; allow 15 mm on each side and 15 mm between each tool.
Step 1, plan dimensions and cut panels

A typical 10-tool roll: spine panel 450 × 300 mm (17 3/4 × 12 in); pocket strip 450 × 120 mm (17 3/4 × 4 3/4 in). Cut both panels with a sharp leatherwork knife against a metal straight-edge. Cut the tie strap: one piece 500 × 20 mm (19 3/4 × 3/4 in). Bevel and burnish all exposed edges on all three pieces before assembly.
Step 2, mark and stitch the pocket strip to the spine

Align the bottom edge of the pocket strip to the bottom edge of the spine panel, grain sides facing out. Mark the stitch line 3 mm (1/8 in) from the bottom edge. Punch the stitch holes through both layers with a stitching chisel. Saddle-stitch the bottom seam the full width of the roll. Stitch the two side edges of the pocket strip to the spine panel. These three seams form the outer envelope of the pocket area.

Mark the vertical division lines across the pocket strip, spacing them to suit the tools. Stitch each division from the bottom seam to the top edge of the pocket strip, through both the pocket strip and the spine. Start and end each division seam with a backstitch through the last two holes to lock the thread. Check each pocket by sliding a tool in before stitching the next division.

Skive (thin) the last 30 mm (1 1/4 in) of one end of the tie strap on the flesh side to reduce bulk at the attachment point. Position the thinned end at the centre of the spine back, 40 mm (1 1/2 in) from the right-hand edge. Punch four holes in a square pattern and stitch or rivet through both the strap and the spine. Bevel and burnish the free end of the tie strap to a rounded tip.
Tools must be able to be removed with a two-finger grip; pockets narrower than 20 mm (3/4 in) trap smaller handles. Test with each tool before stitching the division.
Cutting the spine panel too short for the rolled diameter.

A fully loaded roll has a diameter of 60 to 80 mm (2 1/2 to 3 in). The spine must be wide enough to wrap the roll and still allow the tie strap to close; add 150 mm (6 in) to the total tool width for the overlapping flap.
